sound_type: sharp chick-ick -- thin, staccato, doubled two-note call. Higher-pitched than Hairy's peek! Drums softly and infrequently. Bark-flaking foraging nearly silent. E=4.
ecosystem: Ponderosa pine forest, montane mixed-conifer. Dry-side Cascades. Mature ponderosa pine required for foraging, nesting, and winter food (pine seeds).
season: Resident (year-round). Non-migratory. Sedentary in ponderosa pine habitat.
family: Picidae (7th consecutive: 107-113 -- TIES Corvidae septet record)

coordinates.description: Reference for eastern Cascades ponderosa pine habitat. White-headed Woodpeckers found in mature ponderosa pine forests on the east side of the Cascades in Washington.

taxonomy_note.description: Seventh consecutive Picidae (107-113). TIES the Corvidae septet record (100-106). Dryobates albolarvatus -- same genus as Hairy Woodpecker (110) and Downy Woodpecker (111). Three Dryobates in four degrees. The only North American woodpecker with a white head on a black body -- the inverse pattern. Ponderosa pine specialist. Bark flaker, not driller. First dry-side Cascades specialist. E=4.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Picidae count: 10 entries (Northern Flicker 21-22, Red-breasted Sapsucker 54, Pileated Woodpecker 54, Acorn Woodpecker 107, Pileated Woodpecker laughing 108, Pileated Woodpecker drumming 109, Hairy Woodpecker 110, Downy Woodpecker 111, Lewis's Woodpecker 112, White-headed Woodpecker 113)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Dryobates from Greek drys ('tree') + bates ('one who treads') -- 'tree-treader.' albolarvatus from Latin albus ('white') + larvatus ('masked') -- 'white-masked.' The white-masked tree-treader.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Dryobates -- same genus as Hairy Woodpecker (degree 110) and Downy Woodpecker (degree 111). Three Dryobates in four degrees: continental generalist (Hairy), suburban generalist (Downy), ponderosa pine specialist (White-headed). The genus spans the full range of ecological specialization.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Piciformes. Seventh consecutive (107-113). TIES the Corvidae septet record (100-106, seven). The longest Picidae streak in the engine equals the longest overall streak.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Picidae continues for a seventh consecutive degree. TIES the all-time family-streak record (Corvidae septet, 100-106, seven consecutive). The question: does 114 break the record?

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 3,000-7,000 Hz. Higher-pitched than most Picidae vocalizations in the streak.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 4,000-6,000 Hz. The sharp, thin quality places it in the upper range of woodpecker calls.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Chick-ick call: 3,000-7,000 Hz. Sharp, thin, staccato. Drum: soft, moderate speed, infrequent.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Chick-ick call: 0.2-0.4 seconds. Very brief. Drum: brief, soft.
song_metrics.note_structure: CHICK-ICK: Two notes in quick succession, second slightly lower in pitch. Doubled, staccato quality. Distinctive once learned.
song_metrics.note_types: Chick-ick call (primary, contact/territorial), soft drum (infrequent). The chick-ick is the diagnostic vocalization.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Simple doubled call. No complex patterns or repeated phrases.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp. Thin. Staccato. Higher-pitched than Hairy's peek! and less resonant than Downy's pik. A clipped, quick quality.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- MODERATE energy. Not particularly loud. Calls infrequently. Bark-flaking foraging nearly silent. Drums softly. One of the least acoustically conspicuous NA woodpeckers.
song_metrics.tempo: The chick-ick is quick -- two rapid notes. No sustained vocalizations.
song_metrics.context: CONTACT AND TERRITORIAL. Chick-ick used for contact between mates and low-key territorial signaling. Less vocally persistent than Hairy or Downy.
song_metrics.key_property: SHARP CHICK-ICK -- thin, staccato, doubled. E=4. BARK FLAKER -- pries bark scales, does not heavy-drill. PONDEROSA PINE SPECIALIST. INVERSE PATTERN -- white head, black body, only NA woodpecker with this pattern. Seventh consecutive Picidae (107-113) -- TIES CORVIDAE RECORD. First dry-side Cascades specialist.

plumage.overall: Medium-sized woodpecker. Entirely white head (face, crown, nape) on entirely black body (back, wings, tail, breast, belly). White wing patch visible in flight. Males: small red nape patch (absent in females). No barring, no stripes. The inverse pattern -- a negative image of typical woodpecker plumage.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 113: (1) ALL-WHITE HEAD -- unique among NA woodpeckers. (2) ALL-BLACK BODY -- no barring or stripes. (3) WHITE WING PATCH in flight. (4) Small red nape patch in males only. (5) Inverse pattern: simple, bold, binary.
plumage.flight_pattern: Typical undulating woodpecker flight (unlike Lewis's direct flight at 112). White wing patches flash in flight -- field mark.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized woodpecker. Body length 21-23 cm. Wingspan 40-43 cm. Weight 55-65 g. White head, black body. Slightly smaller than Hairy Woodpecker.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 113: white-headed, black-bodied woodpecker in ponderosa pine forest on the east side of the Cascades. No other woodpecker has this pattern. Unmistakable.
physical_description.field_id: White head on black body. Ponderosa pine habitat. Quiet, methodical. Hitches along trunks and large branches. Bark flaking. Chick-ick call. Undulating flight with white wing patches.

nodes.title: The Inverse Woodpecker -- White Head, Black Body
nodes.description: The only North American woodpecker with a white head on an entirely black body. A negative image. No barring, no stripes. The simplicity of the pattern is startling. Unmistakable if seen, though quiet behavior means it is often overlooked. Visual distinctiveness contrasts with acoustic modesty.

nodes.title: Bark Flaker, Not Driller
nodes.description: Pries bark scales rather than drilling into solid wood. Uses bill as a lever to expose insects beneath loose bark. In winter, extracts ponderosa pine seeds from cones. Quiet foraging -- nearly silent compared to drilling woodpeckers. Precision over power.

nodes.title: Ponderosa Pine Dependency
nodes.description: Requires mature ponderosa pine for foraging substrate (thick plated bark), winter food (pine seeds from cones), and nesting (cavity excavation in snags). Ponderosa pine range in PNW = White-headed Woodpecker range. No ponderosa, no White-headed Woodpecker.

nodes.title: TIES Corvidae Septet Record -- 7th Consecutive Picidae
nodes.description: Seven consecutive Picidae (107-113) ties seven consecutive Corvidae (100-106). The two longest family streaks in the engine. Both in the second half of the rotation. Both comprehensive family surveys: from generalists to specialists, from common to unusual. Does 114 break the record?

nodes.title: First Dry-Side Cascades Specialist
nodes.description: First species strongly associated with east-side Cascades ecology. Ponderosa pine forests of the eastern slopes. Two consecutive ponderosa pine specialists (Lewis's 112, White-headed 113). The engine documents the eastern flank of the PNW ecosystem.

nodes.title: Three Dryobates in Four Degrees (110-113)
nodes.description: Hairy (110), Downy (111), White-headed (113). Three species of genus Dryobates. Continental generalist, suburban generalist, ponderosa pine specialist. Same genus spans full ecological range. Remarkable genus concentration in the Picidae streak.

cross_references.relationship: PONDEROSA PINE SPECIALISTS. Two consecutive (112-113). Lewis's flycatches (aerial), White-headed bark-flakes (surface). Both dry-side Cascades. Both E=4. Different foraging, shared habitat dependency.
SAME GENUS (Dryobates). Hairy: continental generalist (E=4). White-headed: ponderosa pine specialist (E=4). Same genus, vastly different ecological breadths.
SAME GENUS (Dryobates). Three Dryobates in four degrees (110-111, 113). Downy: suburban generalist. White-headed: ponderosa specialist. Full ecological range within one genus.
FORAGING CONTRAST. Pileated: heavy excavation, deep drilling, massive cavities (E=6). White-headed: bark flaking, prying, minimal drilling (E=4). Power vs. precision.
PICIDAE STREAK ENDPOINTS. Acorn at start (107), White-headed ties record (113). Colonial/granary/oak vs. solitary/bark-flaking/ponderosa. Both food specialists in different tree systems.
